headline: Risk-off flips the script: an attack on tankers in the Strait of Hormuz sent oil surging ~3%, AI chips got 'pummeled' overnight, and India's five-day rally snapped — all one day before TCS opens earnings season.
market_story: The friendly backdrop cracked in a single move. Projectiles struck a Qatari gas tanker and a Saudi crude supertanker in the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow sea lane that carries about a fifth of the world's oil — breaking the fragile US–Iran truce and jolting US crude (WTI) up about 3.3% to ~$72.75. For India, which imports ~85% of its oil, a rising crude price is the exact opposite of the cheap-oil tailwind that had powered a five-session rally, so the Nifty and Sensex snapped their streak and gapped down (Nifty ~24,286, −0.5%; Sensex ~77,836). The other half of the risk-off story came from Wall Street on Tuesday, where the crowded AI-and-chip trade was 'pummeled' — a semiconductor gauge sank over 4.5% (Micron −4.7%, plus AMD, Broadcom and Marvell) — dragging the Dow off its record and the Nasdaq down 1.16%. That combination — a fresh war premium in oil plus a wobbling AI trade — pushed the 'fear gauge' VIX up ~3.6% to ~16, pulled Bitcoin below $63,000 (ETF investors keep cashing out), and left gold roughly flat near $4,136. The rupee held ~95.2 for now, but a sustained oil spike is a fresh headwind. All of this lands the day before Tata Consultancy Services (TCS) opens India's Q1 earnings season on 9 July — a reality check the market must pass while an oil shock and a US chip selloff play out at the same time. Among your favorites: Apple ($310.66, −0.64%) gave back a little but held its uptrend; Nvidia ($196.93, +0.71%) actually eked out a gain while its chip peers fell, yet stays stuck below its 50-day line; Reliance (₹1,292.2, −1.24%) slid to a fresh 52-week-low zone; and Bitcoin (~$62,538, −2.28%) lost the $63k support it had reclaimed.

board.name: S&P 500
board.read: US large-caps slipped 0.45% at Tuesday's close as the AI-chip trade reversed and oil rose — the broad market following tech lower, off last week's highs.

board.name: Nasdaq
board.read: The tech-heavy index led the fall (−1.16%) as money rotated OUT of AI names again — a semiconductor gauge sank over 4.5% in a day.

board.name: Dow Jones
board.read: The Dow gave back 131 points, slipping off its record after hitting a fresh intraday high — a pause after a fast run, dragged by chips and rising oil.

board.name: Nifty 50
board.read: India's benchmark gapped down and snapped a five-day rally as the Hormuz oil spike hit the cheap-crude tailwind that had driven the gains.

board.name: Sensex
board.read: The 30-stock index fell in step with the Nifty on the oil shock and weak Asian cues, one day before TCS opens earnings season.

board.name: Bank Nifty
board.read: Banks eased with the market; still above their 200-day average, but higher oil (and stickier inflation) trims the odds of near-term RBI rate cuts that help lenders.

board.name: Bitcoin
board.read: Slid back below the $63k support it had reclaimed, near a one-year low — ETF outflows and a no-cut Fed keep the downtrend intact.

board.name: Ethereum
board.read: Fell harder than Bitcoin (−2.6%) — the higher-beta crypto drops more on risk-off days; still in a clear downtrend below its key averages.

board.name: Gold
board.read: Roughly flat — surprisingly muted given the Hormuz scare, as the safe-haven bid flowed into oil rather than gold; still below its 50/200-day lines.

board.name: Crude Oil (WTI)
board.read: The day's big mover: up ~3.3% after tankers were hit in Hormuz. RSI ~39 — off oversold, with a fresh 'war premium' now priced in.

board.name: USD/INR
board.read: The rupee is marginally firmer near 95.2/$, but a sustained oil spike widens India's import bill and is a fresh headwind for the currency.

board.name: VIX (volatility)
board.read: Wall Street's 'fear gauge' ticked up ~3.6% to ~16 as the oil shock and chip selloff nudged hedging demand higher — still low, but no longer falling.

themes.note: The Hormuz tanker attack put a war premium back into crude (WTI +3.3%), lifting oil producers — but pressuring oil-importing economies and fuel-consuming sectors.
The crowded chip trade reversed hard — a US semis gauge fell 4.5% (Micron, AMD, Broadcom) — as investors questioned whether AI profits justify sky-high valuations.
Bitcoin below $63k and Ether −2.6% on steady ETF outflows and a Fed that won't cut — crypto trading like a risk asset, not a safe haven.
Relatively resilient into TCS's results — a weaker rupee helps dollar-earners, but a US AI-trade wobble is a sentiment risk for the sector.

what_to_watch.title: Oil spikes as tankers are hit in the Strait of Hormuz
key_points: WTI crude jumped ~3.3% to ~$72.75 on the attack
A Qatari LNG tanker and a Saudi supertanker were struck
Breaks the US–Iran truce that reopened Hormuz in June
India imports ~85% of its oil — the biggest single loser
what_to_watch.gist: Projectiles hit two tankers in the Strait of Hormuz — the sea gate for about a fifth of the world's oil — reviving a 'war premium' (extra price paid to insure against supply cutoffs) and sending US crude up ~3.3% to ~$72.75 after weeks of decline.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: For India, oil is the single biggest lever on the whole economy: it feeds petrol prices, inflation, the government's subsidy bill and the rupee. A sustained spike widens the import bill and can cool the equity rally that cheap oil had fuelled — which is exactly why the Nifty gapped down today.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: The cheap-oil tailwind that drove India's five-day rally is at risk; watch whether this is a one-day scare or the start of a lasting premium.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether more ships are hit or Hormuz traffic is disrupted, and whether WTI holds above ~$72 or fades back toward the high-$60s.

key_terms.definition: A narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman carrying roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil and much of its gas; there is no easy way around it.
The extra amount buyers pay for oil to insure against conflict cutting off supply, even before any barrel is actually lost.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: For an oil-importing country, a single event at a chokepoint can move the whole market — geography and geopolitics, not just how much is pumped, set the energy price and ripple into inflation, currency and stocks.

what_to_watch.title: TCS opens India's earnings season tomorrow
key_points: Results after market hours on 9 July; interim dividend on the table
Revenue seen roughly flat at ~₹71,700–72,300 crore
Margins may dip ~150 bps on wage hikes and AI investment
The market's key read on AI: threat or demand driver?
what_to_watch.gist: Tata Consultancy Services (TCS), India's largest IT-services firm, reports June-quarter (Q1 FY27) results after the close on 9 July. Analysts expect roughly flat revenue near ₹72,000 crore and an operating margin around 23.8% — squeezed by annual wage hikes and AI spending — plus a likely interim dividend.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: TCS opens the earnings season and sets the tone for the whole IT sector, which has been under a cloud all year over whether AI will disrupt it or drive new demand. Earnings are the reality check that decides whether prices built on cheap oil and domestic buying have a foundation.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: Strong deal wins and upbeat AI commentary would validate the IT rally and lift sentiment; a soft print would hit IT hardest and puncture the 'India as a calm harbour' story.
what_to_watch.watch: Revenue growth, the operating-margin number, large-deal wins, BFSI (banking) demand, and management's AI commentary — plus the dividend.

key_terms.definition: The share of revenue left as profit after running costs — a key gauge of how efficiently a company turns sales into earnings.
Hundredths of a percentage point; a 150-bps margin drop means 1.5 percentage points lower.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: The first big earnings report of a season carries outsized weight — it resets expectations for every peer that reports after it, so one company's numbers can move an entire sector.

what_to_watch.title: US AI-chip trade reverses; first Warsh Fed minutes Wednesday
key_points: A US semiconductor gauge sank over 4.5% on 7 July
Dow off its record; Nasdaq −1.16%, Tesla −4%
Wednesday brings the new Fed chair's first meeting minutes
A read-through to Indian IT and foreign flows
what_to_watch.gist: Wall Street's crowded AI-and-chip trade sold off hard on Tuesday — Micron −4.7%, with AMD, Broadcom and Marvell also lower — pulling the Dow off its record and the Nasdaq down 1.16%. Attention now turns to Wednesday's minutes from the June Federal Reserve meeting, the first under new chair Kevin Warsh.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: The AI trade is the load-bearing wall of this market; when it wobbles, the whole index shakes. The Fed minutes are the first real window into how the Warsh-era Fed reasons — a hawkish, inflation-first tone would firm the dollar (keeping the rupee soft) and pressure rate-sensitive tech worldwide.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: A shaky US AI trade dampens sentiment for Indian IT right before TCS reports; a firm dollar keeps foreign investors cautious on India.
what_to_watch.watch: Wednesday's Fed minutes for any hint on rate cuts, and whether the chip selloff deepens or snaps back.

key_terms.definition: The detailed written record of a Federal Reserve meeting, released weeks later; traders mine it for clues about future rate moves.
Investors shifting money out of one group of stocks (here, AI chips) into others — which can pull a whole index down even when the economy is fine.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: When a market is led by one theme, that theme is both its engine and its biggest risk — a reversal in the crowded trade can drag the index down regardless of the wider economy.

what_to_watch.title: Bitcoin loses $63k support as ETF outflows persist
key_points: Bitcoin −2.3% to ~$62,540, near a one-year low
US spot Bitcoin ETFs saw ~$4.5bn of June outflows
A no-cut Fed removes crypto's cheap-money fuel
Ether fell harder, −2.6% to ~$1,750
what_to_watch.gist: Bitcoin slipped about 2.3% to ~$62,540, giving back the $63,000 support it had briefly reclaimed. The drivers: heavy US ETF outflows (~$4.5bn in June), capital rotating between crypto and the AI trade, and a Fed that has removed expectations of 2026 rate cuts — which keeps 'safe' bond yields attractive versus non-yielding Bitcoin.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Bitcoin is trading like a high-risk tech asset, not 'digital gold' — it fell with AI chips on the same rate logic. Persistent ETF outflows show institutions trimming rather than buying dips, and a tight Fed removes the tide that lifted crypto in past cycles.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: Crypto remains a barometer of speculative risk appetite; until flows and the Fed turn, rallies are likely to be sold.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether Bitcoin holds the ~$62,000 support area, ETF flow data, and the tone of Wednesday's Fed minutes.

key_terms.definition: A stock-exchange-listed fund that holds actual Bitcoin, letting investors buy exposure through a normal brokerage account.
A price zone where buyers have previously stepped in; a decisive break below it can invite more selling.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: An asset's label and its behaviour can differ — Bitcoin is marketed as a hedge but here dropped alongside risky tech; watch what an asset does, not just what it's called.

what_to_watch.title: The rupee, oil and a below-normal monsoon — India's inflation math
key_points: Rupee steady ~95.2/$, but oil spike is a fresh headwind
IMD sees a below-normal monsoon (~92% of average)
CPI inflation rose to 3.9% in May, led by food
Higher oil + patchy rain trims RBI rate-cut room
what_to_watch.gist: The rupee is holding near 95.2 per dollar, just off record lows, but today's oil spike is a new pressure point because pricier crude widens India's import bill. Layer on a below-normal monsoon forecast (~92% of the long-period average) and May CPI inflation ticking up to 3.9%, and two of India's cheap-cost tailwinds — fuel and food — are both at risk.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: The RBI has already paused after a sharp rate-cutting cycle, citing 'monsoon-related risks.' Higher oil and food prices together could keep inflation firm and force the central bank to stay on hold — which weighs on rate-sensitive banks, real estate and consumer stocks.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: A weaker rupee and firmer inflation mean the RBI has less room to cut; that's a slow headwind for rate-sensitive parts of the market.
what_to_watch.watch: The crude price, July–August rainfall distribution, the next CPI print, and the RBI's tone at its coming meeting.

key_terms.definition: The total a country pays for goods it buys abroad; for India, oil is the single biggest item, so crude's price swings the whole bill.
The Consumer Price Index measures how fast household prices are rising; food is its biggest component in India.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: Currencies, commodities and weather feed straight into the interest-rate outlook — for a big oil-importing, farm-heavy economy, a rising oil price and a weak monsoon can tie a central bank's hands.

global_summary.us: US stocks fell on 7 July as the crowded AI-chip trade reversed — a semiconductor gauge sank over 4.5%, the Dow slipped off its record (−0.25%) and the Nasdaq lost 1.16%. Wednesday's first Fed minutes under chair Kevin Warsh are the next signal.
global_summary.india: The Nifty (~24,286) and Sensex (~77,836) snapped a five-day rally and gapped down as the Hormuz oil spike hit the cheap-crude tailwind; IT held up relatively well ahead of TCS's results on 9 July.
global_summary.crypto: Bitcoin fell ~2.3% below $63,000 and Ether −2.6%, dragged by persistent ETF outflows and a Fed that has ruled out 2026 cuts — crypto is trading as a risk asset.
global_summary.macro: Crude jumped ~3.3% to ~$72.75 after tankers were hit in the Strait of Hormuz; gold was roughly flat near $4,136, the VIX ticked up to ~16, and the rupee held ~95.2/$.

catalysts_today.event: Crude reaction to Strait of Hormuz tanker attacks
June FOMC minutes — first under chair Kevin Warsh
TCS Q1 FY27 results + interim dividend

favorites.name: Apple Inc.
favorites.whats_new: Gave back a small −0.64% (from $312.66) as the broad AI-trade wobble pulled US tech lower, but held firmly inside its uptrend just below the $315 record. The pullback is normal profit-taking, not a trend change.

news.headline: US stocks fall as AI-chip trade reverses; Dow slips off record

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~$4.56T) in Technology, at a rich trailing P/E of ~38 (a lower forward P/E of ~32 signals expected profit growth). Revenue is growing ~17% a year and it keeps ~27¢ of every sales dollar as profit; beta ~1.10 means it moves roughly with the market. Dividend yield ~0.3%.
favorites.technicals: In a healthy uptrend, rated Strong Buy: price sits above both its 50-day (~$295) and 200-day (~$271) averages, with all 10 moving averages bullish. RSI ~61 is neutral (room to run), MACD is positive, but short-term oscillators (Stochastic, CCI, Williams %R) are overbought — a caution flag after a fast run near the 96th percentile of its 52-week range.
favorites.catalysts: Fiscal Q3 earnings later this month; product-cycle and AI-feature news; broad US tech sentiment and Wednesday's Fed minutes.
favorites.risks: Overbought short-term signals leave room for a pullback; a daily close below the ~$295 50-day average would warn the uptrend is stalling. A firm dollar and slowing US consumer are slow headwinds.

favorites.name: NVIDIA Corp.
favorites.whats_new: Quietly resilient — Nvidia rose +0.71% even as the wider chip complex was 'pummeled' (Micron −4.7%, AMD and Broadcom lower). But it remains a laggard, stuck below its 50-day average and rated Sell, so the bounce doesn't change the sideways picture.

news.headline: Semiconductor gauge sinks over 4.5% as AI trade reverses

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~$4.77T) in Semiconductors, trailing P/E ~30 with a much lower forward P/E of ~15 — the market expects earnings to keep surging (revenue is growing ~85% a year, and it keeps ~63¢ of every sales dollar as profit). Beta ~2.2 makes it far more volatile than the market; dividend yield ~0.5%.
favorites.technicals: Sideways and rated Sell: price is caught between its 50-day (~$210) and 200-day (~$191) averages, so the trend could break either way. RSI ~44 is neutral, MACD momentum is negative, and 7 of 10 moving averages are bearish. Support to hold ~$193; a close back above ~$210 would be the first sign the trend is turning up.
favorites.catalysts: The direction of the US AI/chip trade, Wednesday's Fed minutes, and its own next earnings; any AI-demand headline moves it fast given its beta.
favorites.risks: It sits right on the $191 200-day line that defines its longer trend — a decisive break below would be bearish. High beta means outsized moves in a risk-off tape; the AI-valuation debate is the key overhang.

favorites.name: Reliance Industries
favorites.whats_new: Fell −1.24% (from ₹1,314.9) to a fresh 52-week-low zone as the broad market gapped down; ironically the oil spike is a mixed blessing for a refiner (higher crude can squeeze refining margins). It stays in a textbook downtrend, rated Strong Sell.

news.headline: Indian markets snap five-day rally, gap down as crude spikes

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~₹17.5T) in Energy, at a moderate trailing P/E of ~22 (forward P/E ~18 implies expected growth). Revenue is growing ~12% a year and it keeps ~8¢ of every sales dollar as profit; beta ~0.18 makes it much steadier than the market. Dividend yield ~0.5%.
favorites.technicals: Downtrend, rated Strong Sell: price is below both its 50-day (~₹1,338) and 200-day (~₹1,417) averages, with all 10 moving averages bearish, at just the 10th percentile of its 52-week range. RSI ~43 is neutral and MACD/CCI hint at a possible oversold bounce; support to hold ~₹1,279, and a close back above ~₹1,338 would be the first sign of a turn.
favorites.catalysts: Crude and refining-margin trends, any news on retail/Jio/new-energy verticals, and broad India market direction into earnings season.
favorites.risks: Trading near 52-week lows with a confirmed downtrend; a break below ~₹1,279 support opens more downside. Higher crude can pressure refining economics even as it's an energy name.

favorites.name: Bitcoin
favorites.whats_new: Lost the $63k support it had reclaimed, falling −2.28% to ~$62,538 near a one-year low as ETF outflows and a risk-off tape (oil spike, chip selloff) weighed. The bigger downtrend is unchanged.

news.headline: Bitcoin falls below $63,000 as ETF outflows persist

favorites.fundamentals: The largest cryptocurrency by value (~$1.26T market cap). Bitcoin pays no yield, so its appeal falls when 'safe' bond returns are high — and a Fed that has ruled out 2026 cuts keeps those yields attractive versus non-yielding crypto.
favorites.technicals: Downtrend, rated Neutral overall: price is below both its 50-day (~$66,196) and 200-day (~$74,478) averages, at just the 8th percentile of its 52-week range, and trend strength is strong (ADX ~28) — down-moves tend to persist. RSI ~48 is neutral and MACD shows a faint bullish crossover; support to hold ~$62,167, resistance ~$63,289.
favorites.catalysts: US spot-ETF flow data, Wednesday's Fed minutes, and the risk-on/off mood in AI/tech that crypto now tracks closely.
favorites.risks: Near a one-year low with strong downward trend strength; a break below ~$62k support risks a fresh leg down. Persistent institutional ETF outflows and a no-cut Fed are the main overhangs.

risks: Oil shock: a sustained Hormuz war premium would widen India's import bill, pressure the rupee and revive inflation — the biggest cross-book risk today.
AI-trade fragility: another leg down in US semiconductors would drag global tech and Indian IT sentiment right as TCS reports.
Earnings disappointment: a soft TCS print on 9 July could puncture the IT rally and the broader 'calm India' narrative.
Fed surprise: a hawkish tone in Wednesday's first Warsh-era minutes would firm the dollar, soften the rupee and pressure rate-sensitive assets.
Monsoon/inflation: a below-normal monsoon plus firmer food and fuel prices could keep the RBI on hold, weighing on rate-sensitive banks, real estate and consumer names.
disclaimer: For information only. Not financial advice. Data may be delayed and can be inaccurate; verify before acting.
